author: @martinhoult
text: So this is an off topic idea that I hope you might like ? A Heat exchanger is the newest thing for home heating at the moment and although efficient they can cost a great deal to run if you don’t upgrade the heating pipes radiators hot water cylinder’s and then adequately update the insulation and add better triple glazing etcetera ?  Now a large sand batterie if built large enough can hold heat for a very long time so a 2 cubic metre box would hold a lot of heat ? Say you put an oil filled pipe with a circulating pump in to heat up the sand and run it on an off peak night cycle and set a thermostat on it to say 150 degrees c over a number of nights or  weeks you would get this up to temperature and very cheaply too ? Now put another pipe and pump to run this into a hot water cylinder to heat a your hot water and be your heating?? Both thermostat controlled? You could add to this a dump system from some solar panels to add even more heating the summer which would make it even cheaper to run that or a solar heat collecting system? Also with the right construction you could put this into your attic and spread the load on the joists by making it say 100 or 200 cm high internally add the insulation on the out side and you could build it over 10 or twenty metres for the same cubic metres of storage?? And same topic different idea why can’t you put your heat exchanger in a large box with one way in and out air vents and heat the space with a night storage heater again off peak heat and therefore heat everything much much more efficiently and cheaply? You could even make a double glazed heat box around it in direct sunlight to solar heat it as well?

author: @spydocs
text: Brilliant as always RMS.  I’ve probably missed this in previous videos… why is speed over torque so important for domestic generation? Is it a reflection of the variability of wind/hydro speeds? Is it an electrical phenomena (volts better than amps?). Is it to do with availability of reclaimed parts? Many thanks for any insights, Simon

replies.author: @leostarling5746
replies.text:
Given constant strength magnetic field, such as produced by permanent magnets, output is directly dependent on speed. You can play volts against amps with wire thickness but their product (Watts) will always be proportional to speed. Thus high rotor speed is indispensable for useful power generation. When it can be achieved without gearing, then significant mechanical losses are avoided, which translates to even more output (per input). Thus Rob's direct rim generation is a great idea, though it has its practical limits:  to make a large generator, you will need lots of magnets and wire. After thinking about it,  I advise using lots of magnets, which are cheap, on a large diameter rotor. Then make a stator that covers only a small part of the circle, thus keeping the amount of (serpentine) coil within reasonable limits. This has the same benefit as gearing, without its losses.
For example, if Rob used in this demo one of his rim generators with greater diameter, he would have obtained even higher output (assuming the little turbine could spin it at the same rotational speed).

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text:
The recent video about the Archimedes screw does go into torque vs speed. 
My understanding is that it depends on the source of energy and the way it is harvested.
Water in a stream isn't fast but has a lot of torque, and is fairly predictable while wind is rather unpredictable and doesn't have much torque, but with a horizontal wind turbine it produces very high rotational speed.
After seeing Robert's video about recycling vertical fans (squirrel cage) to make wind mills, I've been experimenting with it and found that even though they have greater torque than their horizontal counterpart, it's still preferable to use a low torque generator as it allows harvesting more energy if the windmill can keep spinning when the wind slows down to a light breeze. Finding a motor which can provide at least 5V at 2 RPM is proving difficult though.

replies.author: @leostarling5746
replies.text:
@VeniceInventors this is true but I was analyzing the electric generation end. It  always needs maximum possible speed, unless you have  much stronger electromagnets whose back emf will match your torque.  With weak permanent magnets, you only have the speed to play with and you will only achieve that match (maximum power output) at very high speeds. So, if you have slow torquey motive source, like low head water, you must gear it up somehow. Though to a lesser extent,  the same applies to the wind as well. There you might be able to get away with rim generation on big diameter rotors, in decent winds.
Rob makes demos that 'something can be generated' but if you ever want to generate useful power, you must consider this.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
= to produce more torque - more  load needs to be on the system - Matching maximum torque of the system at any RPM is key to approaching a good efficiency at steady state output. (ue te most out for the least in - materials and hydro resource - , with such a small generator stage (low lods, these babies are nearly up to "freestream speed " of the jet- "Betts limit" - or somesuch more acceptable metric for hydro turbines (constrained flow)  will "indicate the mechanical load" which will produce the most power (peak) out of the system - now to convert all that to electric output..  Using an unloaded the rotor  the velocity  will approach the jet speed - steady state, but mot load the cups up well, ie, operating fairly inefficiently.

(with a given PM generator - speed rules - as per other comments)

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text: @leostarling5746 Thanks for bringing up rim generation. It is something I could easily try as there is a flat disk on the squirrel cages where magnets can fit and  be fine tuned with the coil to match my requirements. Using new and recycled motors is convenient but leaves a lot to chance.
